cluster.title: Top 50 Pairs Exercise Library
cluster.definition: Exercise cards that require exactly two people. The partner supplies timing, cueing, resistance, support, pressure, catch/throw exchange, or live movement stimulus. Drills requiring groups of three or more are intentionally excluded.
source_basis: exercise_card_details_for_llm.md: Card v2 authoring workflow, canonical phase keys, taxonomy, publish gates, dosage/scaling/safety/pairing/regimen fields, and anti-patterns.
Purpose.txt was requested and searched for but was not found in the accessible File Library results for this session.
User focus: paired exercises requiring two people, such as medicine ball tosses with trunk twists and two-person tug-of-war.
cluster.primary_authoring_format: camelCase Card v2 with taxonomy key + weight tags
excluded: group drills requiring three or more people
chaotic partner contests without quality gates
fatigue games mislabeled as speed or skill

categories.name: Partner Medicine Ball Throws, Catches, and Rotational Power
categories.focus: Two-person medicine-ball drills that train throw timing, trunk rotation, catch readiness, relocation, deceleration, and clean force transfer without becoming a random conditioning game.

categories.name: Paired Strength, Carries, and Manual Resistance
categories.focus: Two-person resistance drills where the partner supplies controlled load, drag, assistance, or opposition so the athlete builds force, posture, grip, and tissue tolerance with clear role switching.

categories.name: Partner Core, Balance, and Perturbation Control
categories.focus: Two-person drills that use touch, taps, band pull, ball exchange, or partner pressure to train trunk control, balance, anti-rotation, and contact recovery.

categories.name: Reactive Agility, Chase, Mirror, and Decision Drills
categories.focus: Paired movement games where the second athlete supplies the live stimulus. The goal is perception-action quality, clean acceleration, braking, and reset behavior, not group chaos or fatigue.

categories.name: Contact, Wrestling, Gymnastics Support, and Cooperative Locomotion
categories.focus: Two-person contact and cooperative body-control drills for low stance, hand fighting, crawling, supported locomotion, assisted transitions, and shared force management.

cards.name: Partner Medicine Ball Chest Pass
cards.category: Partner Medicine Ball Throws, Catches, and Rotational Power
cards.cardSummary: Partner Medicine Ball Chest Pass is a two-person paired exercise for horizontal upper-body power, trunk stiffness, visual tracking, and safe partner timing. Use it when the goal is partner timing, shared force, or live stimulus quality rather than group chaos or fatigue chasing.
cards.bestPlacement: Use after warm-up and movement prep, before heavy strength or conditioning. Keep volume low, rest fully, and stop when speed, accuracy, posture, or decision quality drops.
cards.description: Partner stands face-to-face and passes a light medicine ball explosively from the chest with a clean catch and reset.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: jump_throw_explosive_power. Coach Partner Medicine Ball Chest Pass as pair-specific work: define roles, cap partner force, use clear stop commands, and preserve quality before competition.
cards.athleteLanguage: Communicate, match your partner, and own the reset.
cards.selectionRationale: Selected because it requires exactly two people and directly trains horizontal upper-body power, trunk stiffness, visual tracking, and safe partner timing with a partner-created constraint.
cards.whyItWorks: The partner creates a constraint that a solo drill cannot fully reproduce: live timing, shared resistance, external pressure, or a real visual cue. That makes Partner Medicine Ball Chest Pass valuable for horizontal upper-body power, trunk stiffness, visual tracking, and safe partner timing because the athlete must organize posture, force, perception, and communication at the same time.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Output because the task expresses speed, power, throwing, acceleration, cutting, or reactive athleticism at high quality while fresh.
cards.commonMisuse: The main mistake is letting the partner make the drill random, too heavy, or too competitive. Keep the input matched to the athlete and stop before accuracy, posture, spacing, or communication breaks. Do not extend sets until breathing or fatigue decides the outcome; this is speed/power quality, not conditioning.
cards.scalingGuidance: Progress only one variable at a time: partner force, implement load, speed, distance, unpredictability, range, duration, or contact intensity. Regress by removing the live partner input first.

pair_profile.partner_input: cue, toss, resistance, assistance, pressure, or perturbation as defined by the card
pair_profile.group_size_note: Designed for pairs only; do not require groups of three or more.

reactive_output_profile.stimulus_type: partner visual, tactile, or ball cue
reactive_output_profile.quality_rule: stop before timing, speed, catch quality, or decision quality degrades

coachingExecution.movement_description: Partner stands face-to-face and passes a light medicine ball explosively from the chest with a clean catch and reset.
setup: Pair athletes by size, skill, maturity, and ability to follow stop commands.
Define the athlete role, partner role, allowed contact/resistance, and the stop word before the first rep.
Set safe spacing and clear the area of obstacles; use cones, mat, line, band, or ball as needed.
execution_steps: Athlete performs: Partner stands face-to-face and passes a light medicine ball explosively from the chest with a clean catch and reset.
Partner supplies only the agreed cue, toss, resistance, support, or perturbation and stays attentive to the athlete's posture.
Reset after each rep or short work window, then switch roles so both partners receive the same quality exposure.
coach_cues: Talk before force.
Match the partner, do not beat the partner.
Clean rep, clean reset.
Stop on sloppy spacing or posture.
common_faults: Partner adds too much force or surprise
Athlete rushes the rep to win instead of solving the movement problem
Spacing drifts and the pair loses a safe lane
Breath holding, shrugging, or trunk collapse under partner input
Reps continue after speed or decision quality drops
breathing_cues: Do not hold breath to survive partner force
coachingExecution.quality_gate: The rep counts only if both partners keep the agreed role, safe spacing, communication, posture, and controlled reset.

dosage.coaching_note: Dose both roles. One set is complete only after both athletes have performed the athlete role unless the card is assigned to a specific role.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use a cooperative partner, smallest range, and lightest implement/resistance. Teach roles first; only count reps of Partner Medicine Ball Chest Pass when both athletes can communicate and stop safely.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Add slightly more range, distance, or partner cue variability while keeping the partner force predictable and non-surprising.
scaling.teen: Use standard spacing and moderate intent. Match partners by size, maturity, and control; rotate roles before fatigue changes behavior.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with slower tempo, lighter partner pressure, shorter distances, and more rest. Regress to solo or band-supported versions if coordination breaks.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress one variable at a time: implement load, partner force, approach speed, cue unpredictability, distance, or work density. Do not progress all at once.
scaling.older_adult: Use stable surfaces, light partner contact, low speed, and optional hand support. Prioritize confidence, communication, and pain-free control over challenge.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use only with medical clearance and comfort. Avoid breath holding, heavy contact, abdominal pressure, high-speed cutting, and prolonged supine positions; choose standing or supported variations.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender adjustment. Pair by size, strength, confidence, maturity, and communication rather than gender.
pairsWellBefore: Conditioning only if quality is preserved
doNotUseWhen: Partners cannot follow stop commands or keep the input controlled
The area is crowded or spacing cannot be protected
The athlete has pain, fear, dizziness, or unsafe compensation during the solo version
The session goal requires quiet individual practice rather than partner interaction
mediaInternalNotes: Film both roles, not only the athlete role.
Demo the partner input separately before full-speed reps.
Show the stop command and role switch in any media clip.

readiness_checks: Both partners understand the roles, stop command, and allowed contact or resistance level
Athlete can demonstrate the base position without partner force first
No pain, dizziness, panic steps, or loss of safe spacing during the practice rep
contraindications: Acute pain or injury in the loaded joints
Partner mismatch that creates unsafe size, speed, or maturity differences
Athlete cannot follow stop commands or maintain safe spacing
common_substitutions: Solo version of the same pattern

coachingExecution.movement_description: Athlete holds an athletic stance while partner gives light shoulder bumps from pre-agreed directions.
setup: Pair athletes by size, skill, maturity, and ability to follow stop commands.
Define the athlete role, partner role, allowed contact/resistance, and the stop word before the first rep.
Set safe spacing and clear the area of obstacles; use cones, mat, line, band, or ball as needed.
execution_steps: Athlete performs: Athlete holds an athletic stance while partner gives light shoulder bumps from pre-agreed directions.
Partner supplies only the agreed cue, toss, resistance, support, or perturbation and stays attentive to the athlete's posture.
Reset after each rep or short work window, then switch roles so both partners receive the same quality exposure.
coach_cues: Talk before force.
Match the partner, do not beat the partner.
Clean rep, clean reset.
Stop on sloppy spacing or posture.
common_faults: Partner adds too much force or surprise
Athlete rushes the rep to win instead of solving the movement problem
Spacing drifts and the pair loses a safe lane
Breath holding, shrugging, or trunk collapse under partner input
Perturbation becomes a challenge contest instead of controlled exposure
breathing_cues: Do not hold breath to survive partner force
coachingExecution.quality_gate: The rep counts only if both partners keep the agreed role, safe spacing, communication, posture, and controlled reset.

dosage.coaching_note: Dose both roles. One set is complete only after both athletes have performed the athlete role unless the card is assigned to a specific role.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use a cooperative partner, smallest range, and lightest implement/resistance. Teach roles first; only count reps of Partner Shoulder Bump Balance when both athletes can communicate and stop safely.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Add slightly more range, distance, or partner cue variability while keeping the partner force predictable and non-surprising.
scaling.teen: Use standard spacing and moderate intent. Match partners by size, maturity, and control; rotate roles before fatigue changes behavior.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with slower tempo, lighter partner pressure, shorter distances, and more rest. Regress to solo or band-supported versions if coordination breaks.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress one variable at a time: implement load, partner force, approach speed, cue unpredictability, distance, or work density. Do not progress all at once.
scaling.older_adult: Use stable surfaces, light partner contact, low speed, and optional hand support. Prioritize confidence, communication, and pain-free control over challenge.

cards.name: Partner Band Waist Tug Hold
cards.category: Partner Core, Balance, and Perturbation Control
cards.cardSummary: Partner Band Waist Tug Hold is a two-person paired exercise for center-of-gravity control, hip recovery, foot pressure, and safe pull exposure. Use it when the goal is partner timing, shared force, or live stimulus quality rather than group chaos or fatigue chasing.
cards.bestPlacement: Use after access work and after high-speed/high-power priorities. Keep partner inputs controlled; the set ends when posture, pain-free control, or safe recovery breaks.

cards.athleteLanguage: Communicate, match your partner, and own the reset.
cards.selectionRationale: Selected because it requires exactly two people and directly trains center-of-gravity control, hip recovery, foot pressure, and safe pull exposure with a partner-created constraint.
cards.whyItWorks: The partner creates a constraint that a solo drill cannot fully reproduce: live timing, shared resistance, external pressure, or a real visual cue. cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Resilience because the partner creates controlled perturbation, pressure, eccentric demand, or support that develops stability, braking, trunk control, and tissue durability.

cards.description: One athlete changes direction at low speed while partner shadows with defensive spacing and posture.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: perception_action_skill. Coach Partner Shadow Defense Drill as pair-specific work: define roles, cap partner force, use clear stop commands, and preserve quality before competition.
cards.athleteLanguage: Communicate, match your partner, and own the reset.
cards.selectionRationale: Selected because it requires exactly two people and directly trains spacing, hip angle, live opponent reading, and repeatable defensive footwork with a partner-created constraint.
cards.whyItWorks: The partner creates a constraint that a solo drill cannot fully reproduce: live timing, shared resistance, external pressure, or a real visual cue. That makes Partner Shadow Defense Drill valuable for spacing, hip angle, live opponent reading, and repeatable defensive footwork because the athlete must organize posture, force, perception, and communication at the same time.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Movement Intelligence because the partner supplies a live cue, timing problem, or shared coordination task while the athlete is still fresh enough to learn.
cards.commonMisuse: The main mistake is letting the partner make the drill random, too heavy, or too competitive. Keep the input matched to the athlete and stop before accuracy, posture, spacing, or communication breaks.
cards.scalingGuidance: Progress only one variable at a time: partner force, implement load, speed, distance, unpredictability, range, duration, or contact intensity. Regress by removing the live partner input first.

pair_profile.partner_input: cue, toss, resistance, assistance, pressure, or perturbation as defined by the card
pair_profile.group_size_note: Designed for pairs only; do not require groups of three or more.

reactive_output_profile.stimulus_type: partner visual, tactile, or ball cue
reactive_output_profile.quality_rule: stop before timing, speed, catch quality, or decision quality degrades

coachingExecution.movement_description: One athlete changes direction at low speed while partner shadows with defensive spacing and posture.
setup: Pair athletes by size, skill, maturity, and ability to follow stop commands.
Define the athlete role, partner role, allowed contact/resistance, and the stop word before the first rep.
Set safe spacing and clear the area of obstacles; use cones, mat, line, band, or ball as needed.
execution_steps: Athlete performs: One athlete changes direction at low speed while partner shadows with defensive spacing and posture.
Partner supplies only the agreed cue, toss, resistance, support, or perturbation and stays attentive to the athlete's posture.
Reset after each rep or short work window, then switch roles so both partners receive the same quality exposure.
coach_cues: Talk before force.
Match the partner, do not beat the partner.
Clean rep, clean reset.
Stop on sloppy spacing or posture.
common_faults: Partner adds too much force or surprise
Athlete rushes the rep to win instead of solving the movement problem
Spacing drifts and the pair loses a safe lane
Breath holding, shrugging, or trunk collapse under partner input
breathing_cues: Do not hold breath to survive partner force
coachingExecution.quality_gate: The rep counts only if both partners keep the agreed role, safe spacing, communication, posture, and controlled reset.

dosage.coaching_note: Dose both roles. One set is complete only after both athletes have performed the athlete role unless the card is assigned to a specific role.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use a cooperative partner, smallest range, and lightest implement/resistance. Teach roles first; only count reps of Partner Shadow Defense Drill when both athletes can communicate and stop safely.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Add slightly more range, distance, or partner cue variability while keeping the partner force predictable and non-surprising.
scaling.teen: Use standard spacing and moderate intent. Match partners by size, maturity, and control; rotate roles before fatigue changes behavior.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with slower tempo, lighter partner pressure, shorter distances, and more rest. Regress to solo or band-supported versions if coordination breaks.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress one variable at a time: implement load, partner force, approach speed, cue unpredictability, distance, or work density. Do not progress all at once.
scaling.older_adult: Use stable surfaces, light partner contact, low speed, and optional hand support. Prioritize confidence, communication, and pain-free control over challenge.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use only with medical clearance and comfort. Avoid breath holding, heavy contact, abdominal pressure, high-speed cutting, and prolonged supine positions; choose standing or supported variations.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender adjustment. Pair by size, strength, confidence, maturity, and communication rather than gender.
pairsWellBefore: Conditioning only if quality is preserved
doNotUseWhen: Partners cannot follow stop commands or keep the input controlled
The area is crowded or spacing cannot be protected
The athlete has pain, fear, dizziness, or unsafe compensation during the solo version
The session goal requires quiet individual practice rather than partner interaction
mediaInternalNotes: Film both roles, not only the athlete role.
Demo the partner input separately before full-speed reps.
Show the stop command and role switch in any media clip.

readiness_checks: Both partners understand the roles, stop command, and allowed contact or resistance level
Athlete can demonstrate the base position without partner force first
No pain, dizziness, panic steps, or loss of safe spacing during the practice rep
contraindications: Acute pain or injury in the loaded joints
Partner mismatch that creates unsafe size, speed, or maturity differences
Athlete cannot follow stop commands or maintain safe spacing
common_substitutions: Solo version of the same pattern

Match the partner, do not beat the partner.

cards.name: Partner-Assisted Inverted Row Hold
cards.category: Contact, Wrestling, Gymnastics Support, and Cooperative Locomotion
cards.cardSummary: Partner-Assisted Inverted Row Hold is a two-person paired exercise for pulling strength, scapular position, trunk stiffness, and partner-scaled assistance. Use it when the goal is partner timing, shared force, or live stimulus quality rather than group chaos or fatigue chasing.
cards.bestPlacement: Use after skill, speed, and power work. Pair partners by size, control, and maturity so force stays progressive instead of chaotic.
cards.description: Athlete holds an inverted row angle while partner assists or adds light manual resistance at the upper back or hands.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: upper_body_pull_strength. Coach Partner-Assisted Inverted Row Hold as pair-specific work: define roles, cap partner force, use clear stop commands, and preserve quality before competition.
cards.athleteLanguage: Communicate, match your partner, and own the reset.
cards.selectionRationale: Selected because it requires exactly two people and directly trains pulling strength, scapular position, trunk stiffness, and partner-scaled assistance with a partner-created constraint.
cards.whyItWorks: The partner creates a constraint that a solo drill cannot fully reproduce: live timing, shared resistance, external pressure, or a real visual cue. That makes Partner-Assisted Inverted Row Hold valuable for pulling strength, scapular position, trunk stiffness, and partner-scaled assistance because the athlete must organize posture, force, perception, and communication at the same time.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the partner creates progressive resistance, assistance, load, or shared force that builds strength and structural reserve.
cards.commonMisuse: The main mistake is letting the partner make the drill random, too heavy, or too competitive. Keep the input matched to the athlete and stop before accuracy, posture, spacing, or communication breaks. Do not turn manual resistance or carries into a race; strength work needs repeatable force and safe role switching.
cards.scalingGuidance: Progress only one variable at a time: partner force, implement load, speed, distance, unpredictability, range, duration, or contact intensity. Regress by removing the live partner input first.

pair_profile.group_size_note: Designed for pairs only; do not require groups of three or more.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Athlete holds an inverted row angle while partner assists or adds light manual resistance at the upper back or hands.
setup: Pair athletes by size, skill, maturity, and ability to follow stop commands.
Define the athlete role, partner role, allowed contact/resistance, and the stop word before the first rep.
Set safe spacing and clear the area of obstacles; use cones, mat, line, band, or ball as needed.
execution_steps: Athlete performs: Athlete holds an inverted row angle while partner assists or adds light manual resistance at the upper back or hands.
Partner supplies only the agreed cue, toss, resistance, support, or perturbation and stays attentive to the athlete's posture.
Reset after each rep or short work window, then switch roles so both partners receive the same quality exposure.
coach_cues: Talk before force.
Match the partner, do not beat the partner.
Clean rep, clean reset.
Stop on sloppy spacing or posture.
common_faults: Partner adds too much force or surprise
Athlete rushes the rep to win instead of solving the movement problem
Spacing drifts and the pair loses a safe lane
Breath holding, shrugging, or trunk collapse under partner input
Manual resistance becomes jerky instead of smooth and matched
breathing_cues: Do not hold breath to survive partner force
coachingExecution.quality_gate: The rep counts only if both partners keep the agreed role, safe spacing, communication, posture, and controlled reset.

dosage.coaching_note: Dose both roles. One set is complete only after both athletes have performed the athlete role unless the card is assigned to a specific role.
